unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! In this unit you will use English to talk about maths in real life. You will count and name big numbers, all the way up to a million. You will learn the words for addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. You will measure things around you — their size, height, weight, and the distance between them — using words like meter and centimeter. You will also look for patterns and straight lines. Maths is everywhere: in the market, in the kitchen, and on the way to school. By the end, you will be ready to solve a number puzzle, just like Isaac and Hannah do in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few maths words each day.
Work through the eight short lessons in the Grammar file.
Read the Story file, “The Mystery of the Million Shells”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Learners investigating numbers, shapes and measurement

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A word or symbol that shows how many things there are.
practiceSentences: My favourite number is seven.
Each locker has a different number on its door.
Which number is missing from this counting song?
The teacher said, "Double the number and add one."
I counted every number from one to one hundred.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - u - m - b - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'number', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very big number: 1,000,000, which is one thousand thousands.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are more than a million stars in the sky.
practiceSentences: There are more than a million stars in the sky.
A million seconds is more than eleven days.
How many zeros does one million have?
Samira said, "I wish I had a million books."
I imagine a million ants marching in a line.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - l - l - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'million', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that is true and can be proven.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is a fact that a week has seven days.
practiceSentences: It is a fact that a week has seven days.
It is a fact that spiders have eight legs.
Can you share one fact about the moon?
Adam said, "A fact can be checked and proven."
My favourite fact is that octopuses have three hearts.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fact', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A design or sequence that repeats.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We saw a colour pattern: red, blue, red, blue.
practiceSentences: We saw a colour pattern: red, blue, red, blue.
The tiles make a pattern of stars and squares.
What comes next in this shape pattern?
Sami said, "I made a pattern with red and gold beads."
I found a number pattern counting by fives.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - a - t - t - e - r - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'pattern', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Not bent or curved; in a line.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Draw a straight line across the page.
practiceSentences: Draw a straight line across the page.
Use a ruler to keep your lines straight.
Is the road to the market straight or curved?
The teacher said, "Stand in a straight line, please."
I folded the paper along a straight edge.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - r - a - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'straight', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Putting numbers together to find a total.
practiceSentences: We did addition today: 3 + 2 = 5.
Addition joins two numbers to make a total.
Can you solve this addition problem in your head?
Maya said, "Addition is easy with counting beads."
I practised addition with bottle tops at home.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - d - d - i - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'addition', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Taking one number away from another.
practiceSentences: In subtraction, 6 minus 2 equals 4.
Subtraction tells us how many are left over.
Is subtraction the opposite of addition?
Theo said, "Subtraction shows how many sweets remain."
I solved ten subtraction sums without any mistakes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - b - t - r - a - c - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'subtraction', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Adding the same number many times.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We use multiplication to find how many apples are in 4 boxes.
practiceSentences: We use multiplication to find how many apples are in 4 boxes.
Multiplication is a fast way of adding equal groups.
Which multiplication table are you learning now?
Samira said, "Multiplication helps me count eggs in trays."
I made a chart to learn my multiplication tables.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - u - l - t - i - p - l - i - c - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'multiplication', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Splitting a number into equal parts.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Division helps us share dates fairly.
practiceSentences: Division helps us share dates fairly.
Division shares things equally between friends.
Can division split twelve dates among four children?
Sami said, "Division is sharing done with numbers."
I used division to hand out the art paper fairly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - v - i - s - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'division', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To find the size or amount of something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will measure how tall the table is.
practiceSentences: We will measure how tall the table is.
We measure the bean plants every Monday morning.
Can you measure the desk with this ruler?
The teacher said, "Measure twice before you cut."
I measure my height against the kitchen door.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - a - s - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'measure', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How big or small something is.
practiceSentences: What size are your shoes?
The shoes come in every size at the market.
What size is your reading book, big or small?
Adam said, "These boxes are the same size."
I sorted the buttons by size and colour.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'size', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A unit used to measure length.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The table is two meters long.
practiceSentences: The table is two meters long.
One meter is one hundred centimeters long.
Is the doorway more than a meter wide?
Maya said, "My jump measured one whole meter!"
I cut a meter of string for the kite.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'meter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How tall something or someone is.
practiceSentences: My height is 120 centimeters.
The nurse checked each child's height and wrote it down.
What is the height of the classroom door?
Theo said, "My height went up two centimeters!"
I marked my height on the wall chart.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'height', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The weight of the watermelon is 3 kilos.
practiceSentences: The weight of the watermelon is 3 kilos.
The scales show the weight of the flour.
Can you guess the weight of this pumpkin?
Samira said, "The weight of my bag surprised me."
I compared the weight of a feather and a stone.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - e - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'weight', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How far something is from another thing.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The distance from school to my house is short.
practiceSentences: The distance from school to my house is short.
The map shows the distance between the two towns.
What distance can you run without stopping?
Adam said, "The distance to the market is short."
I measured the distance across the playground in steps.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - t - a - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'distance', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and say big numbers, up to a million.
Use the maths words addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
Measure the size, height, weight, and distance of things around you.
Talk about straight lines and repeating patterns.
Give clear instructions using imperatives (“Measure the height.”).
Say what you own with have got, and what you can do with can / can't.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about the future with will, and explain reasons using because.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Maths Is Everywhere
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Numbers are all around us. When Samira helps her mother at the market, she uses addition to add the prices. When they share the dates with four cousins, they use division. Samira measures the size of the flour bag and checks its weight. On the way home, she counts her steps to find the distance to their door. Maths helps us every day!

readings.title: A Pattern Poem
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One, two, buckle my shoe,
Three, four, count some more,
Five, six, shapes and sticks,
Straight and round, patterns found!

readings.title: The Measuring Challenge
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Good morning, class! Today we will measure three things. First, measure the height of your desk. Next, measure the distance from the door to the window. Then, find the weight of your school bag. Write each number in your book. You can use a meter stick. Ready? Begin!”

readings.title: Numbers Big and Small
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Ten. One hundred. One thousand. Ten thousand. One hundred thousand. One million! A million is a very big number — it is one thousand thousands.”

readings.title: The Mystery of the Million Shells
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was a hot afternoon in Cape Coast. The sea breeze blew through the windows of Class 3B.
“Today,” said Teacher Nadia, “we have a challenge!” The board read: “Can you find the right pattern to solve the number puzzle?” The class clapped.
Isaac looked at the table. There were seashells, string, rulers, and bowls. “Each team will measure the size, weight, and distance between the shells,” said Teacher Nadia. “Use your eyes, your hands, and your maths.”
“What is the first step?” asked Hannah.
“Measure the height of your tallest shell,” said the teacher.
Isaac's team got to work. “This is a straight shell,” said Hannah. “That one is curved.”
“This shell is 2 meters from the bowl,” said Isaac. “Let's write that down,” said Hannah. The teams wrote their numbers in their books:
Height: 8 cm.   Distance: 2 meters.   Weight: 150 grams.
“Let's do some addition and subtraction,” said Isaac. Hannah smiled. “And don't forget multiplication and division!”
Suddenly, Hannah pointed at the board. “There's a clue!” she shouted. It read: “There are more than 500 shells. Fewer than a million. Use a pattern. Count carefully.”
Isaac counted the shells in one bowl. “There are 25 in this one. And there are 10 bowls.”
“25 times 10 is …?” Hannah asked. “Multiplication!” said Isaac. “That's 250.”
“And what if we add the shells on the table?” Hannah asked. Isaac replied, “Then it's over 300. Maybe 400.” “Still far from a million,” Hannah laughed.
“Can you measure this shell?” Isaac asked. “I can't,” Hannah said. “It's too small, and the line isn't straight.” “Use the other ruler,” said Isaac. “It has better units.” “Good idea,” Hannah said. “I will do it now.”
Teacher Nadia walked by. “These numbers are correct. But remember, maths is not only about numbers. It is about facts and smart thinking.”
“Shall we check our pattern again?” Hannah asked. “Yes,” said Isaac. “Let's see if our division is equal.”
Later that day, each team shared their answers. Hannah stood up and said: “We used multiplication and addition. We counted by fives. We measured distance in meters and height in centimeters. Our total number of shells is 375.”
Teacher Nadia smiled. “You all did well. The answer is … 375 shells. Hannah's team found the right pattern.” The class clapped.
Isaac whispered, “Teamwork is good because we learn more together.”
The teacher gave a final task. “Write a report using these words: number, measure, pattern, distance. Add one new maths fact.” Isaac wrote: “We measured the distance from the bowl. We found a pattern in the numbers. One fact I learned: 25 × 10 = 250.”
As they packed up, Hannah said, “I've got a new idea for next week. Let's count the school chairs.” Isaac laughed. “Only if we stop before a million!”

grammar.title: Present Simple and Imperatives
grammar.explanation: The Present Simple tells about things that happen often or facts that are always true, like “The sun rises in the east.” An imperative is a command or instruction, like “Measure the height.” Both help us speak and write clearly, especially in maths.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Present Simple: subject + verb (add s for he, she, it). “I count the shells.” / “She counts the shells.”
Imperative: just the verb, with no subject. “Count the numbers.” / “Measure the table.”
Negative imperative: use Don't. “Don't run in the hall.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He measure the desk.”  ✗
Fix: add s for he/she/it: “He measures the desk.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: For an imperative, just say the verb — you are the boss giving one clear order. For he, she, it in Present Simple, don't forget the s!
grammar.practice: ______ (Count) the numbers on the board. (imperative) | She ______ (measure) the rope every morning. (present simple) | ______ (Measure) the height of your desk. (imperative) | We ______ (add) the prices at the market. (present simple)

grammar.title: ‘Have got’ for Ownership
grammar.explanation: We use have got to talk about things we own or have. It helps us tell people about our belongings, like “I've got a ruler.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
I / you / we / they have got (’ve got). “We have got ten bowls.”
He / she / it has got (’s got). “She has got a new ruler.”
Negative: haven't got / hasn't got. “They haven't got any shells.”
Question: Have you got …? “Have you got a meter stick?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He have got a bicycle.”  ✗
Fix: use has got for he/she/it: “He has got a bicycle.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of ‘have got’ like a treasure chest — you open it to show what you own. He, she, it → has got.
grammar.practice: I ______ (have got / haven't got) a new maths book. I love it! | She ______ (has got / hasn't got) a ruler, so she cannot draw a straight line. | We ______ (have got / haven't got) a lot of homework tonight. | ______ you ______ (Have / got) a meter stick?

grammar.title: Can / Can't for Ability
grammar.explanation: We use can to say what we are able to do, and can't for what we are not able to do. It helps us talk about our skills, like “I can measure the distance.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
subject + can + base verb. “You can measure the distance.”
subject + can't + base verb. “You can't subtract letters.”
Question: Can + subject + base verb? “Can you count to a million?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I can swimming.” / “I can measuring.”  ✗
Fix: use the base verb after can: “I can swim.” / “I can measure it.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “can” as a superhero cape. “I can” = cape on, you have the power. “I can't” = cape off.
grammar.practice: I ______ (can / can't) count to one hundred. It is easy! | She ______ (can / can't) reach the top shelf; she is too short. | ______ (Can / Can't) you measure this shell for me, please? | We ______ (can / can't) subtract letters — only numbers!

grammar.title: Demonstratives (This / That)
grammar.explanation: This and that point to things. We use this for something near us and that for something farther away. This makes it clear which thing we mean.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
This (near, one thing). “This is a straight line.”
That (far, one thing). “That pattern is complex.”
These (near, many) / those (far, many). “These are my rulers.” / “Those are your shells.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “That is my pencil,” when the pencil is in your hand.  ✗
Fix: it is near you, so use this: “This is my pencil.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Say it like a rhyme: “This is near, that is clear (over there)!”
grammar.practice: ______ (This / That) shell in my hand is very small. | ______ (This / That) tree over there is very tall. | Look at ______ (this / that) straight line I drew here! | Do you see ______ (this / those) mountains far in the distance?

grammar.title: Future with ‘Will’
grammar.explanation: We use will to talk about the future — promises, plans, and predictions. It tells what has not happened yet, like “I will measure the meter tomorrow.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
subject + will + base verb. “I will multiply the numbers.”
Negative: will not (won't). “I won't forget the answer.”
Question: Will + subject + base verb? “Will you measure it tomorrow?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I will multiplied the numbers.”  ✗
Fix: use the base verb after will: “I will multiply the numbers.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “will” as a magic word for time travel to tomorrow. “I will” is your ticket to the future!
grammar.practice: I ______ (read) a new maths book next week. | They ______ (measure) the classroom tomorrow. | She ______ (count) the shells later. | We ______ (not / forget) to check the pattern.

grammar.title: Short Future Responses + Question Forms + Cause and Effect with ‘Because’
grammar.explanation: Short future responses let us answer quickly about the future. We say I will to agree, and I won't to say no. They make our answers clear and confident.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
To say yes to a future action: “I will!”
To say no: “I won't.” (won't = will not)
You can use other subjects too: “She will.” / “They won't.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: writing the negative as “I not will.”  ✗
Fix: the short negative is I won't: “I won't.”  ✓
grammar.practice: Answer with “I will” or “I won't”: | A: “Will you help me measure the desk?”  B: “______, I'd love to!” | A: “Will you count all the way to a million?”  B: “______, that would take too long!” | A: “Will you check the pattern again?”  B: “______, good idea.” | Lesson 7:  Question Forms | What it means and why it matters | Questions help us find information. Good learners ask lots of questions! There are Yes/No questions and Wh- questions (what, where, when, why, who, how). | How to form it | Yes/No: start with a helping verb. “Can you count?” / “Is the line straight?” | Wh-: start with a question word. “What is the distance?” / “How many shells are there?” | Suggestions: use Shall. “Shall I measure the size?” | A common mistake | Mistake: “You can play?” (subject and verb not swapped)  ✗ | Fix: swap them for a question: “Can you play?”  ✓ | Memory tip | For a question, flip the subject and the helping verb: “You are” → “Are you?” | Practice 7 | Write the missing question word or helping word: | ______ is the distance from the door to the window? (asks about a thing) | ______ you measure this shell for me? (Yes/No, about ability) | ______ many bowls are on the table? (asks about number) | ______ we check our pattern again? (a suggestion) | Lesson 8:  Cause and Effect with ‘Because’ | What it means and why it matters | We use because to explain WHY something happens. It joins the effect (what happened) with the cause (the reason). This makes our writing stronger, like “I added a number because the pattern was not complete.” | How to form it | effect + because + cause. “I checked again because the answer looked wrong.” | You can also start with the cause: “Because it was raining, we stayed inside.” | A common mistake | Mistake: leaving out the reason: “I added a number.” (Why?)  ✗ | Fix: add the reason with because: “I added a number because the pattern was not complete.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Think of because as the glue that sticks two ideas together: what happened + why. | Practice 8 | Finish each sentence with a reason using “because”: | We worked as a team ______. | I used multiplication ______. | Hannah could not measure the shell ______.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Measure and Tell
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose something in your home. Measure it, then say:
“This is a ______. Its height is about ______.”
“Its weight is about ______.”
“The distance from it to the door is about ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use a measuring word (meter, centimeter, kilo)? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise measure and tell and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Maths in the Market
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Imagine you are shopping. Practise saying:
“I will buy three bags. (using will)”
“I've got ten shillings. (using have got)”
“Can you measure the flour for me, please? (using can)”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise maths in the market and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess My Pattern
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person says the start of a pattern: “Two, four, six, ______?”
The other person guesses the next number and says the rule: “Eight! You are adding two.”
Try shape patterns too: “circle, square, circle, square, ______?” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise guess my pattern and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — How-To Instructions
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four steps to measure the height of your table. Use imperatives (start each step with a verb):
Step 1: Put the meter stick ______.
Step 2: ______.
Step 3: ______.
Step 4: Write down the ______.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about how-to instructions.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — My Maths Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences using these words: number, measure, pattern, distance. Add one maths fact you know. For example:
“I measured the distance from my bed to the door. I found a pattern in the tiles. One fact I know: 5 × 5 = 25.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about my maths report.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Match the Maths Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to the right meaning. Write the letter.
addition        (a) taking one number away from another
subtraction  (b) putting numbers together to get a total
multiplication  (c) splitting into equal parts
division        (d) adding the same number many times
Answers: 1. b   2. a   3. d   4. c
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — True or False (Number Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A million is one thousand thousands.
A straight line is bent and curved.
We use a meter to measure length.
In subtraction, 6 minus 2 equals 5.
Answers: 1. True   2. False (a straight line is NOT bent or curved)   3. True   4. False (6 − 2 = 4)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the best word: height, weight, distance, pattern.
The ______ of the tree is very tall.
The ______ of the watermelon is 3 kilos.
The ______ from school to my house is short.
The red-blue-red-blue design is a colour ______.
Answers: 1. height   2. weight   3. distance   4. pattern
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Count and Multiply
activities.instructionsAndItems:
There are 25 shells in one bowl. There are 10 bowls.
How many shells are there in total? (Use multiplication: 25 × 10)
If you take away 5 shells, how many are left? (Use subtraction.)
Answers: 1. 250 shells   2. 245 shells
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement learning portfolio

assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'number' mean?
quizzes.options: A design or sequence that repeats. | A word or symbol that shows how many things there are. | Not bent or curved; in a line. | Something that is true and can be proven.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A word or symbol that shows how many things there are.

quizzes.question: What does 'million' mean?
quizzes.options: A design or sequence that repeats. | A very big number: 1,000,000, which is one thousand thousands. | Not bent or curved; in a line. | Putting numbers together to find a total.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A very big number: 1,000,000, which is one thousand thousands.
quizzes.explanation: There are more than a million stars in the sky.

quizzes.question: What does 'fact' mean?
quizzes.options: Not bent or curved; in a line. | Putting numbers together to find a total. | Something that is true and can be proven. | Taking one number away from another.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something that is true and can be proven.
quizzes.explanation: It is a fact that a week has seven days.

quizzes.question: What does 'pattern' mean?
quizzes.options: A design or sequence that repeats. | Adding the same number many times. | Putting numbers together to find a total. | Taking one number away from another.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A design or sequence that repeats.
quizzes.explanation: We saw a colour pattern: red, blue, red, blue.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Mystery of the Million Shells': “Today,” said Teacher Nadia, “we have a challenge!” The board read: “Can you find the right pattern to solve the _____ puzzle?” The class clapped.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Today,” said Teacher Nadia, “we have a challenge!” The board read: “Can you find the right pattern to solve the number puzzle?” The class clapped.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Numbers Big and Small': A _____ is a very big number — it is one thousand thousands.”
quizzes.explanation: The text says: A million is a very big number — it is one thousand thousands.”
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Mystery of the Million Shells': Add one new maths _____.” Isaac wrote: “We measured the distance from the bowl.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Add one new maths fact.” Isaac wrote: “We measured the distance from the bowl.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Mystery of the Million Shells': “Today,” said Teacher Nadia, “we have a challenge!” The board read: “Can you find the right _____ to solve the number puzzle?” The class clapped.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Today,” said Teacher Nadia, “we have a challenge!” The board read: “Can you find the right pattern to solve the number puzzle?” The class clapped.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Pattern Poem': One, two, buckle my shoe, Three, four, count some more, Five, six, shapes and sticks, _____ and round, patterns found!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: One, two, buckle my shoe, Three, four, count some more, Five, six, shapes and sticks, Straight and round, patterns found!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Maths Is Everywhere': When Samira helps her mother at the market, she uses _____ to add the prices.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: When Samira helps her mother at the market, she uses addition to add the prices.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the pattern poem with me and clap the beat?”
“Can you play a listening game where you say a number and I write it?”
“Can you say a measuring instruction and let me act it out?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise saying big numbers?”
“Can you play ‘Guess My Pattern’ with me?”
“Can you check if I used the measuring words correctly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check if my how-to steps are in the right order?”
“Can you check my maths report and my spelling?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on saying big numbers?”
“Can you give me a pattern to finish?”
“Is this sentence a fact or not: ______?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you give me three addition and three subtraction questions?”
“What is the difference between multiplication and addition?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me use meters and centimeters correctly?”
“What is the difference between height and distance?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the maths verbs?”
“Can you help me count by 5s and by 10s?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she measures’ but ‘I measure’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise ‘have got’?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A word or symbol that shows how many things there are. My favourite number is seven.
A very big number: 1,000,000, which is one thousand thousands. There are more than a million stars in the sky.
Something that is true and can be proven. It is a fact that a week has seven days.
A design or sequence that repeats. We saw a colour pattern: red, blue, red, blue.
Not bent or curved; in a line. Draw a straight line across the page.
Putting numbers together to find a total. We did addition today: 3 + 2 = 5.
Taking one number away from another. In subtraction, 6 minus 2 equals 4.
Adding the same number many times. We use multiplication to find how many apples are in 4 boxes.
Splitting a number into equal parts. Division helps us share dates fairly.
To find the size or amount of something. We will measure how tall the table is.
